Before we conclude this course and move into the simulation, I just wanted to recap and touch upon a few points. All of these skills are fantastic tools. As long as you don't lose your head, this is always easier said than done, everyone is an emotional beast, including you, including me. If you want to have any hope of coming out on top, you need to work on regulating your emotions. It's very likely that your counterpart will attempt to trigger some sort of emotional response out of you at some point. The first and most basic advice I have to give you is to bite your tongue, obviously not literally, but you have to keep away from knee-jerk, passionate reactions. Avoid rolling your eyes or showing that you're visibly upset. Take a deep breath, pause, and imagine a calm response. Wait until the passion dissipates, collect your thoughts, and use the late-night FM DJ voice with what you say back. The next rule is to never counterattack your opponent when they verbally assault you, instead disarm them by asking a calibrated question. When people feel that they're not in control, they adopt a hostage mentality. Basically, in moments of conflict, they react to their lack of power by either becoming extremely defensive or lashing out. Our primitive fight or flight mechanisms in our brain is engaged and it overwhelms the rational part of our mind, which is we learned previously is why people make choices that are not always in their best interests. On a flip side, don't try to force your counterpart to admit that you're right either. When the conversation gets hostile or emotionally charged, constructive negotiation can't take place. Plus, most people have a team behind them that you have to keep in mind anyway. If you make your immediate counterpart angry, then you won't convince their team either, which leaves you vulnerable. The use of a calibrated question gives your counterpart a chance to assume control and get them thinking about how to solve the issues before them with their own energy while removing the aggression from the conversation. When negotiating with your counterpart, avoid closed-ended, yes-seeking questions. These rarely provide you with insight. Instead, when asking open-ended questions, stick to how and what, these two words imply that you're asking for help, thus giving your counterpart the illusion of control and motivates them to speak expansively and reveal important information. No matter which culture you're in, starting questions with why is always interpreted as an accusation. The only time you should use why is when you want your counterpart to defend a goal that serves you. Remember, your goal is to move your counterpart through to trust-based empathy. You cannot move someone there until you build rapport through tactical, proactive listening. Your first barrier is the state of unbelief. If you don't take that into account, then you'll never even get the chance to start earning their trust because your negotiation will turn into a confrontational showdown. The strategic listener has the control in the conversation. Keep your counterpart talking and revealing information with your calibrated questions. It'll give them the illusion of control even though you're actually directing the conversation towards your own goals.
